attributes.stem: A 60-year-old man with a history of heart failure is admitted to hospital with severe community-acquired pneumonia. He has been treated with intravenous ceftriaxone and azithromycin. At day 3, his condition suddenly deteriorates when he becomes extremely short of breath and his lips turn blue. On auscultation, diffuse fine lung crackles are heard. His oxygen saturation drops to 80% despite on 5L/min nasal cannula. The oxygen therapy is switched to CPAP with pressure of 10 cm H2O and FiO2 of 50%. His ABG result after commencing CPAP shows a PaO2 of 60 mmHg. An echocardiogram shows an ejection fraction of 45% with mild anterior wall hypokinesia. A chest X-ray is performed (Image) result as shown below.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Observe and maintain the CPAP pressure
Transfer patient to ICU and start mechanical ventilation
Refer to extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (ECMO) centre
attributes.explanation:
This patient's respiratory symptoms have worsened (i.e., dyspnoea, central cyanosis) on the third day of his admission. His chest X-ray shows diffuse bilateral patchy opacities, most likely due to extensive interstitial and intra-alveolar oedema secondary to alveoli damage. Although cardiac silhouette can hardly be delineated on this X-ray, his pulmonary oedema is less likely of cardiogenic cause because cardiogenic pulmonary oedema usually follows perihilar distribution (due to congestion of lymphatics) and more commonly affects lower lobes only. Air bronchograms can also be seen in the case of non-cardiogenic pulmonary oedema, and not in the other. Moreover, his echocardiogram only shows mild heart failure, so it would be less consistent with his presentation together with the radiological findings. Lastly, given this patient's PaO2 is 60 mmHg despite on CPAP with FiO2 of 50%, his PaO2/FiO2 is 120.

The patient is most likely experiencing acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) secondary to his underlying pneumonia. According to The Berlin definition of ARDS, all four criteria have to be met in order to confirm the diagnosis of ARDS:

  • Timing: within one week of a known clinical insult or new or worsening respiratory symptoms

  • Chest imaging: bilateral opacities not fully explained by effusions, lobar or lung collapse, or nodules

  • Origin of oedema: respiratory failure not fully explained by cardiac failure or fluid overload

  • Oxygenation: PaO2/FiO2 ≤ 300 mmHg

All four criteria of ARDS are met for this patient.

Severity of ARDS is determined by the PaO2/FiO2:

  • Mild: 200 mmHg > PaO2/FiO2 ≤300 mmHg with positive end-expiratory pressure (PEEP) or continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) ≥5 cm H2O

  • Moderate: 100 mm Hg > PaO2/FiO2 ≤200 mmHg with PEEP ≥5 cm H2O

  • Severe: PaO2/FiO2 ≤ 100 mmHg with PEEP ≥5 cm H2O

Since this patient's PaO2/FiO2 is 120, he is considered having moderate ARDS.

Patients with ARDS are required to be transferred to ICU and start mechanical ventilation under sedation. As a result of extensive damage to the alveoli, the lung becomes less compliant and makes ventilation much more difficult. Therefore, non-invasive ventilation such as CPAP would not be sufficient to maintain the oxygen saturation of the patient. In patients with ARDS, lung protective mechanical ventilation is being used. This is achieved by using low tidal volume ventilation (≤ 6 ml/kg ideal body weight) with high PEEP until plateau pressure < 30 cm H2O. This strategy could help minimise risk of ventilator-induced lung injury (VILI) by limiting the pressure and volume while keeping the alveoli open with positive pressure.

Extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (ECMO) would be considered as the last resort in those with severe ARDS, which is not appropriate for this scenario.

Although this patient is also having concurrent mild heart failure, this is less likely the cause of his hypoxemia because his PaO2 remains low even after commencing CPAP. Also the extensive alveoli involvement as shown in the chest X-ray more likely suggests diffuse lung injury rather than pulmonary oedema as a result of decompensated heart failure. Therefore, IV furosemide is not indicated here.
learningPoints: Patients with diagnosed ARDS should be transferred to ICU and commence mechanical ventilation immediately to maintain oxygenation.
ARDS is diagnosed when all four Berlin criteria are met: timing within 1 week, bilateral opacities on imaging, non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema, and PaO2/FiO2 ≤300 mmHg.
references: Gorman EA, O'Kane CM, McAuley DF. Acute respiratory distress syndrome in adults: diagnosis, outcomes, long-term sequelae, and management. The Lancet. 2022;400: 1157-70. Accessed January 13, 2023. https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(22)01439-8

Papazian L, Aubron C, Brochard L, et al. Formal guidelines: management of acute respiratory distress syndrome. Annals of Intensive Care. 2019;9(69). Accessed January 13, 2023. https://doi.org/10.1186/s13613-019-0540-9

Qadir N, Sahetya S, Munshi L, Summers C, Abrams D, Beitler J, Bellani G, Brower RG, Burry L, Chen JT, Hodgson C, Hough CL, Lamontagne F, Law A, Papazian L, Pham T, Rubin E, Siuba M, Telias I, Patolia S, Chaudhuri D, Walkey A, Rochwerg B, Fan E. An Update on Management of Adult Patients with Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome: An Official American Thoracic Society Clinical Practice Guideline. Am J Respir Crit Care Med. 2024 Jan 1;209(1):24-36. doi: 10.1164/rccm.202311-2011ST. PMID: 38032683; PMCID: PMC10870893. Available from; https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/38032683/
